title: Game Pie
subtitle: Mixed game braised in red wine and juniper, in a shortcrust pastry case
excerpt: A game pie uses whatever is in season or available: typically venison, pheasant breast, and rabbit, braised low and slow in red wine before being enclosed in a shortcrust pastry case and baked. It is served hot in winter and cold in summer.
sourceNotes: Traditional British raised pie tradition. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): game pie with mixed game. Hugh Fearnley-Whittingstall, The River Cottage Meat Book (2004): game pie with red wine and juniper. Cross-referenced across multiple sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: The unbaked pie freezes well for up to 2 months. Bake from frozen at 190°C for 65-70 minutes, covering loosely with foil if the pastry colours too quickly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The braised filling must be made a day ahead to cool completely. Assemble and bake on the day.
recipe.temperatureNote: reduce to 180°C after the first 20 minutes for even cooking

glossaryTerms.definition: Cooking meat slowly in a covered pot with a small amount of liquid at a low oven temperature, usually 150-160°C. Game benefits from braising because the connective tissue in older or harder-worked animals dissolves into gelatin, enriching the sauce and making the meat tender.

The legally defined period during which specific wild species may be shot in Britain. Pheasant runs from October to February; venison depends on the species and gender; grouse from August to December. Outside season, farmed venison is available year-round.

content.text: A game pie is flexible by nature: use what is available. Venison dice, pheasant breast, and rabbit legs are a classic combination, but the method works with any game. The filling is
the day before, cooled completely, and then enclosed in pastry. A warm filling will make the pastry greasy and prevent a clean cut.
is useful: this pie is most naturally made between October and February when pheasant and venison are both available fresh. Outside season, farmed venison and frozen game work perfectly well.

items.prepNote: shoulder or haunch, diced into 3 cm pieces
breasts removed and diced, legs reserved for another use

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C (140°C fan). Fry the
in a casserole until crisp. Remove and set aside. In the same fat, brown the venison and rabbit pieces in batches, then remove. Soften the
and carrots in the casserole for 5 minutes. Add the garlic,
, thyme, and bay leaves. Return the meat and lardons. Pour over the
. Cover and braise for 2 hours. Cool completely; refrigerate overnight.
Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Pick out the bones from the rabbit pieces and discard. Roll two thirds of the

and use it to line a deep 25 cm pie dish. Spoon in the cold filling, leaving 1 cm of headspace. Moisten the pastry rim with water, then lay the remaining pastry as a lid and seal. Trim and crimp the edges. Make a steam vent in the centre. Brush with beaten egg and bake for 20 minutes. Reduce to 180°C and bake for a further 25-30 minutes until deeply golden. Rest for 15 minutes before slicing.

rows.symptom: Filling spills over the pastry rim during baking
rows.cause: Filling was too liquid, or too much was used
rows.fix: Reduce the braising liquid by a third before cooling if it seems very thin. Leave 1 cm of headroom in the dish.

rows.symptom: Rabbit bones in the sliced pie
rows.cause: Small bones are hard to find in the braised meat
rows.fix: Run fingers through the braised rabbit meat carefully before filling the pie. Rabbit leg bones are obvious; the risk is small rib bones.

rows.cause: Filling was too wet or the dish too deep
rows.fix: Use a pie tin rather than a ceramic dish for better base heat conduction. The filling must be cold.

rows.cause: Later-season birds, or venison from a wild animal rather than farmed
rows.fix: Soaking diced game in cold water for 1 hour before browning draws out some blood and reduces strong flavour.

content.text: Game pie has appeared on the British table in winter since at least the Tudor period, made from whatever was shot on the estate. The modern version draws on the French and British traditions equally: a braised filling with wine and herbs, a shortcrust pastry case, and the pleasure of a slice that holds its shape. It is a cold-weather centrepiece.
